revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ukehi]][[ja:うけい]][[zh:宇氣比]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11260316]] Wikidata name: ukehi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{italic title}} {{short description|Shinto divination ritual}}
{{nihongo|'''''Ukehi''''' or '''''Ukei'''''||4=lit. "pledge divination"}} is a Japanese Shinto [[:en:Divination|Divination]] ritual.
==Function and performance== [[:en:Hayashi Oen|Hayashi Oen]], a nineteenth-century practitioner of ''ukehi'', identified six functions of the rite. He claimed it could be used to: * ask for information or messages from the ''[[kami]]'' * establish the will of the ''kami'' * predict the outcome of an event * enervate or animate living beings * manipulate weather conditions * kill one's enemies<ref name="HardacreKern1997">{{cite book|author1=Helen Hardacre|author2=Adam Lewis Kern|title=New Directions in the Study of Meiji Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pXlZoSjXb2QC&pg=PA427|year=1997|publisher=BRILL|isbn=90-04-10735-5|pages=427}}</ref>
The dictates of ''ukehi'' can come as a dream, but more commonly the petitioner would use the ritual to ask a question of the ''kami'' and then await an omen of some sort to confirm their <ref name="Keene2013">{{cite book|author=Donald Keene|title=Emperor of Japan: Meiji and His World, 1852-1912|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VMuPzoc23QMC&pg=PA779|date=13 August 2013|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=978-0-231-51811-6|pages=779}}</ref> response. If nothing happened, it was assumed that the ''kami'' did not favour the proposed course of action.<ref name="HardacreKern1997"/> The questioning of the ''kami'' took the form of an oath or vow.<ref name="Cranston">{{cite book|author=Edwin A. Cranston|title=The Gem-Glistening Cup|date=March 1998 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KqWjwalbmx4C&pg=PA483|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-3157-7|pages=483}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|title=Nichibunken Japan Review: Bulletin of the International Research Center for Japanese Studies|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xfcQAQAAMAAJ|year=1998|publisher=The Center|page=200}}</ref> Sometimes the ritual involved inscribing the choices available on bamboo slips, which were then shaken in a container; whichever slip fell out dictated the appropriate course of action.{{Citation needed|date=October 2014}}
In the novel ''[[:en:Runaway Horses|Runaway Horses]]'', [[:en:Yukio Mishima|Mishima Yukio]] described the procedure of ''ukehi'' as "contain[ing] an element of danger not unlike a footing that could give way at any moment".<ref>{{cite book |last1=三島 Mishima |first1=由紀夫 Yukio |last2=Gallagher |first2=Michael |title=奔馬 Runaway Horses |date=1985 |publisher=[[Charles E. Tuttle Company]] |location=Japan |isbn=978-4805303542}}</ref>
==Notable ''ukehi''== * An ''ukehi'' ritual undertaken by the deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] resulted in the birth of eight more deities.<ref>{{cite book|title=Tenri Journal of Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tQTXAAAAMAAJ|year=1979|publisher=Tenri University Press|page=108}}</ref> * In the late 19th century, Hayashi Oen and his pupil [[:en:Otaguro Tomoo|Otaguro Tomoo]] performed ''ukehi'' several times, and eventually the latter received what he believed was divine authorization to begin the [[:en:Shinpūren rebellion|Shinpūren rebellion]].<ref name="Stokes2000">{{cite book|author=Henry Scott Stokes|title=The Life and Death of Yukio Mishima|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=D-Of7peK2tkC&pg=PA151|date=8 August 2000|publisher=Cooper Square Press|isbn=978-1-4616-2422-6|pages=151–152}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Kotodama]] * [[:en:Flipism|Flipism]], the practice of relying on random outcomes to make decisions
==References== {{reflist}} ‎]] ]]
{{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto]]
[[Category:Divination]]
}}
[[wiktionary:誓占|誓占]]
[[en:Ukehi]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:うけい]] [[zh:宇氣比]] {{wikidata link|Q11260316}}

He claimed it could be used to: * ask for information or messages from the ''[[kami]]'' * establish the will of the ''kami'' * predict the outcome of an event * enervate or animate living beings * manipulate weather conditions * kill one's enemies<ref name="HardacreKern1997">{{cite book|author1=Helen Hardacre|author2=Adam Lewis Kern|title=New Directions in the Study of Meiji Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pXlZoSjXb2QC&pg=PA427|year=1997|publisher=BRILL|isbn=90-04-10735-5|pages=427}}</ref>

If nothing happened, it was assumed that the ''kami'' did not favour the proposed course of action.<ref name="HardacreKern1997"/> The questioning of the ''kami'' took the form of an oath or vow.<ref name="Cranston">{{cite book|author=Edwin A. Tuttle Company]] |location=Japan |isbn=978-4805303542}}</ref>
==Notable ''ukehi''== * An ''ukehi'' ritual undertaken by the deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] resulted in the birth of eight more deities.<ref>{{cite book|title=Tenri Journal of Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tQTXAAAAMAAJ|year=1979|publisher=Tenri University Press|page=108}}</ref> * In the late 19th century, Hayashi Oen and his pupil [[:en:Otaguro Tomoo|Otaguro Tomoo]] performed ''ukehi'' several times, and eventually the latter received what he believed was divine authorization to begin the [[:en:Shinpūren rebellion|Shinpūren rebellion]].<ref name="Stokes2000">{{cite book|author=Henry Scott Stokes|title=The Life and Death of Yukio Mishima|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=D-Of7peK2tkC&pg=PA151|date=8 August 2000|publisher=Cooper Square Press|isbn=978-1-4616-2422-6|pages=151–152}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Kotodama]] * [[:en:Flipism|Flipism]], the practice of relying on random outcomes to make decisions
==References== {{reflist}} ‎]] ]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ukehi]][[ja:うけい]][[zh:宇氣比]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11260316]] Wikidata name: ukehi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{italic title}} {{short description|Shinto divination ritual}}
{{nihongo|'''''Ukehi''''' or '''''Ukei'''''||4=lit. "pledge divination"}} is a Japanese Shinto [[:en:Divination|Divination]] ritual.
==Function and performance== [[:en:Hayashi Oen|Hayashi Oen]], a nineteenth-century practitioner of ''ukehi'', identified six functions of the rite. He claimed it could be used to: * ask for information or messages from the ''[[kami]]'' * establish the will of the ''kami'' * predict the outcome of an event * enervate or animate living beings * manipulate weather conditions * kill one's enemies<ref name="HardacreKern1997">{{cite book|author1=Helen Hardacre|author2=Adam Lewis Kern|title=New Directions in the Study of Meiji Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pXlZoSjXb2QC&pg=PA427|year=1997|publisher=BRILL|isbn=90-04-10735-5|pages=427}}</ref>
The dictates of ''ukehi'' can come as a dream, but more commonly the petitioner would use the ritual to ask a question of the ''kami'' and then await an omen of some sort to confirm their <ref name="Keene2013">{{cite book|author=Donald Keene|title=Emperor of Japan: Meiji and His World, 1852-1912|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VMuPzoc23QMC&pg=PA779|date=13 August 2013|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=978-0-231-51811-6|pages=779}}</ref> response. If nothing happened, it was assumed that the ''kami'' did not favour the proposed course of action.<ref name="HardacreKern1997"/> The questioning of the ''kami'' took the form of an oath or vow.<ref name="Cranston">{{cite book|author=Edwin A. Cranston|title=The Gem-Glistening Cup|date=March 1998 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KqWjwalbmx4C&pg=PA483|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-3157-7|pages=483}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|title=Nichibunken Japan Review: Bulletin of the International Research Center for Japanese Studies|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xfcQAQAAMAAJ|year=1998|publisher=The Center|page=200}}</ref> Sometimes the ritual involved inscribing the choices available on bamboo slips, which were then shaken in a container; whichever slip fell out dictated the appropriate course of action.{{Citation needed|date=October 2014}}
In the novel ''[[:en:Runaway Horses|Runaway Horses]]'', [[:en:Yukio Mishima|Mishima Yukio]] described the procedure of ''ukehi'' as "contain[ing] an element of danger not unlike a footing that could give way at any moment".<ref>{{cite book |last1=三島 Mishima |first1=由紀夫 Yukio |last2=Gallagher |first2=Michael |title=奔馬 Runaway Horses |date=1985 |publisher=[[Charles E. Tuttle Company]] |location=Japan |isbn=978-4805303542}}</ref>
==Notable ''ukehi''== * An ''ukehi'' ritual undertaken by the deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] resulted in the birth of eight more deities.<ref>{{cite book|title=Tenri Journal of Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tQTXAAAAMAAJ|year=1979|publisher=Tenri University Press|page=108}}</ref> * In the late 19th century, Hayashi Oen and his pupil [[:en:Otaguro Tomoo|Otaguro Tomoo]] performed ''ukehi'' several times, and eventually the latter received what he believed was divine authorization to begin the [[:en:Shinpūren rebellion|Shinpūren rebellion]].<ref name="Stokes2000">{{cite book|author=Henry Scott Stokes|title=The Life and Death of Yukio Mishima|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=D-Of7peK2tkC&pg=PA151|date=8 August 2000|publisher=Cooper Square Press|isbn=978-1-4616-2422-6|pages=151–152}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Kotodama]] * [[:en:Flipism|Flipism]], the practice of relying on random outcomes to make decisions
==References== {{reflist}} ‎]] ]]
{{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto]]
[[Category:Divination]]
}}

He claimed it could be used to: * ask for information or messages from the ''[[kami]]'' * establish the will of the ''kami'' * predict the outcome of an event * enervate or animate living beings * manipulate weather conditions * kill one's enemies<ref name="HardacreKern1997">{{cite book|author1=Helen Hardacre|author2=Adam Lewis Kern|title=New Directions in the Study of Meiji Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pXlZoSjXb2QC&pg=PA427|year=1997|publisher=BRILL|isbn=90-04-10735-5|pages=427}}</ref>
The dictates of ''ukehi'' can come as a dream, but more commonly the petitioner would use the ritual to ask a question of the ''kami'' and then await an omen of some sort to confirm their <ref name="Keene2013">{{cite book|author=Donald Keene|title=Emperor of Japan: Meiji and His World, 1852-1912|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VMuPzoc23QMC&pg=PA779|date=13 August 2013|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=978-0-231-51811-6|pages=779}}</ref> response. If nothing happened, it was assumed that the ''kami'' did not favour the proposed course of action.<ref name="HardacreKern1997"/> The questioning of the ''kami'' took the form of an oath or vow.<ref name="Cranston">{{cite book|author=Edwin A. Cranston|title=The Gem-Glistening Cup|date=March 1998 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KqWjwalbmx4C&pg=PA483|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-3157-7|pages=483}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|title=Nichibunken Japan Review: Bulletin of the International Research Center for Japanese Studies|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xfcQAQAAMAAJ|year=1998|publisher=The Center|page=200}}</ref> Sometimes the ritual involved inscribing the choices available on bamboo slips, which were then shaken in a container; whichever slip fell out dictated the appropriate course of action.{{Citation needed|date=October 2014}}
In the novel ''[[:en:Runaway Horses|Runaway Horses]]'', [[:en:Yukio Mishima|Mishima Yukio]] described the procedure of ''ukehi'' as "contain[ing] an element of danger not unlike a footing that could give way at any moment".<ref>{{cite book |last1=三島 Mishima |first1=由紀夫 Yukio |last2=Gallagher |first2=Michael |title=奔馬 Runaway Horses |date=1985 |publisher=[[Charles E. Tuttle Company]] |location=Japan |isbn=978-4805303542}}</ref>
==Notable ''ukehi''== * An ''ukehi'' ritual undertaken by the deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] resulted in the birth of eight more deities.<ref>{{cite book|title=Tenri Journal of Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tQTXAAAAMAAJ|year=1979|publisher=Tenri University Press|page=108}}</ref> * In the late 19th century, Hayashi Oen and his pupil [[:en:Otaguro Tomoo|Otaguro Tomoo]] performed ''ukehi'' several times, and eventually the latter received what he believed was divine authorization to begin the [[:en:Shinpūren rebellion|Shinpūren rebellion]].<ref name="Stokes2000">{{cite book|author=Henry Scott Stokes|title=The Life and Death of Yukio Mishima|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=D-Of7peK2tkC&pg=PA151|date=8 August 2000|publisher=Cooper Square Press|isbn=978-1-4616-2422-6|pages=151–152}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Kotodama]] * [[:en:Flipism|Flipism]], the practice of relying on random outcomes to make decisions
==References== {{reflist}} ‎]] ]]

Cranston|title=The Gem-Glistening Cup|date=March 1998 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KqWjwalbmx4C&pg=PA483|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-3157-7|pages=483}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|title=Nichibunken Japan Review: Bulletin of the International Research Center for Japanese Studies|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xfcQAQAAMAAJ|year=1998|publisher=The Center|page=200}}</ref> Sometimes the ritual involved inscribing the choices available on bamboo slips, which were then shaken in a container; whichever slip fell out dictated the appropriate course of action.{{Citation needed|date=October 2014}}
In the novel ''[[:en:Runaway Horses|Runaway Horses]]'', [[:en:Yukio Mishima|Mishima Yukio]] described the procedure of ''ukehi'' as "contain[ing] an element of danger not unlike a footing that could give way at any moment".<ref>{{cite book |last1=三島 Mishima |first1=由紀夫 Yukio |last2=Gallagher |first2=Michael |title=奔馬 Runaway Horses |date=1985 |publisher=[[Charles E. Tuttle Company]] |location=Japan |isbn=978-4805303542}}</ref>
==Notable ''ukehi''== * An ''ukehi'' ritual undertaken by the deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] resulted in the birth of eight more deities.<ref>{{cite book|title=Tenri Journal of Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tQTXAAAAMAAJ|year=1979|publisher=Tenri University Press|page=108}}</ref> * In the late 19th century, Hayashi Oen and his pupil [[:en:Otaguro Tomoo|Otaguro Tomoo]] performed ''ukehi'' several times, and eventually the latter received what he believed was divine authorization to begin the [[:en:Shinpūren rebellion|Shinpūren rebellion]].<ref name="Stokes2000">{{cite book|author=Henry Scott Stokes|title=The Life and Death of Yukio Mishima|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=D-Of7peK2tkC&pg=PA151|date=8 August 2000|publisher=Cooper Square Press|isbn=978-1-4616-2422-6|pages=151–152}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Kotodama]] * [[:en:Flipism|Flipism]], the practice of relying on random outcomes to make decisions
==References== {{reflist}} ‎]] ]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ukehi]][[ja:うけい]][[zh:宇氣比]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11260316]] Wikidata name: ukehi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{italic title}} {{short description|Shinto divination ritual}}
{{nihongo|'''''Ukehi''''' or '''''Ukei'''''||4=lit. "pledge divination"}} is a Japanese Shinto [[:en:Divination|Divination]] ritual.
==Function and performance== [[:en:Hayashi Oen|Hayashi Oen]], a nineteenth-century practitioner of ''ukehi'', identified six functions of the rite. He claimed it could be used to: * ask for information or messages from the ''[[kami]]'' * establish the will of the ''kami'' * predict the outcome of an event * enervate or animate living beings * manipulate weather conditions * kill one's enemies<ref name="HardacreKern1997">{{cite book|author1=Helen Hardacre|author2=Adam Lewis Kern|title=New Directions in the Study of Meiji Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pXlZoSjXb2QC&pg=PA427|year=1997|publisher=BRILL|isbn=90-04-10735-5|pages=427}}</ref>
The dictates of ''ukehi'' can come as a dream, but more commonly the petitioner would use the ritual to ask a question of the ''kami'' and then await an omen of some sort to confirm their <ref name="Keene2013">{{cite book|author=Donald Keene|title=Emperor of Japan: Meiji and His World, 1852-1912|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VMuPzoc23QMC&pg=PA779|date=13 August 2013|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=978-0-231-51811-6|pages=779}}</ref> response. If nothing happened, it was assumed that the ''kami'' did not favour the proposed course of action.<ref name="HardacreKern1997"/> The questioning of the ''kami'' took the form of an oath or vow.<ref name="Cranston">{{cite book|author=Edwin A. Cranston|title=The Gem-Glistening Cup|date=March 1998 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KqWjwalbmx4C&pg=PA483|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-3157-7|pages=483}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|title=Nichibunken Japan Review: Bulletin of the International Research Center for Japanese Studies|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xfcQAQAAMAAJ|year=1998|publisher=The Center|page=200}}</ref> Sometimes the ritual involved inscribing the choices available on bamboo slips, which were then shaken in a container; whichever slip fell out dictated the appropriate course of action.{{Citation needed|date=October 2014}}
In the novel ''[[:en:Runaway Horses|Runaway Horses]]'', [[:en:Yukio Mishima|Mishima Yukio]] described the procedure of ''ukehi'' as "contain[ing] an element of danger not unlike a footing that could give way at any moment".<ref>{{cite book |last1=三島 Mishima |first1=由紀夫 Yukio |last2=Gallagher |first2=Michael |title=奔馬 Runaway Horses |date=1985 |publisher=[[Charles E. Tuttle Company]] |location=Japan |isbn=978-4805303542}}</ref>
==Notable ''ukehi''== * An ''ukehi'' ritual undertaken by the deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] resulted in the birth of eight more deities.<ref>{{cite book|title=Tenri Journal of Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tQTXAAAAMAAJ|year=1979|publisher=Tenri University Press|page=108}}</ref> * In the late 19th century, Hayashi Oen and his pupil [[:en:Otaguro Tomoo|Otaguro Tomoo]] performed ''ukehi'' several times, and eventually the latter received what he believed was divine authorization to begin the [[:en:Shinpūren rebellion|Shinpūren rebellion]].<ref name="Stokes2000">{{cite book|author=Henry Scott Stokes|title=The Life and Death of Yukio Mishima|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=D-Of7peK2tkC&pg=PA151|date=8 August 2000|publisher=Cooper Square Press|isbn=978-1-4616-2422-6|pages=151–152}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Kotodama]] * [[:en:Flipism|Flipism]], the practice of relying on random outcomes to make decisions
==References== {{reflist}} ‎]] ]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ukehi]][[ja:うけい]][[zh:宇氣比]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ukehi]][[ja:うけい]][[zh:宇氣比]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ukehi]][[ja:うけい]][[zh:宇氣比]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11260316]] Wikidata name: ukehi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{italic title}} {{short description|Shinto divination ritual}}
{{nihongo|'''''Ukehi''''' or '''''Ukei'''''||4=lit. "pledge divination"}} is a Japanese Shinto [[:en:Divination|Divination]] ritual.
==Function and performance== [[:en:Hayashi Oen|Hayashi Oen]], a nineteenth-century practitioner of ''ukehi'', identified six functions of the rite. He claimed it could be used to: * ask for information or messages from the ''[[kami]]'' * establish the will of the ''kami'' * predict the outcome of an event * enervate or animate living beings * manipulate weather conditions * kill one's enemies<ref name="HardacreKern1997">{{cite book|author1=Helen Hardacre|author2=Adam Lewis Kern|title=New Directions in the Study of Meiji Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pXlZoSjXb2QC&pg=PA427|year=1997|publisher=BRILL|isbn=90-04-10735-5|pages=427}}</ref>
The dictates of ''ukehi'' can come as a dream, but more commonly the petitioner would use the ritual to ask a question of the ''kami'' and then await an omen of some sort to confirm their <ref name="Keene2013">{{cite book|author=Donald Keene|title=Emperor of Japan: Meiji and His World, 1852-1912|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VMuPzoc23QMC&pg=PA779|date=13 August 2013|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=978-0-231-51811-6|pages=779}}</ref> response. If nothing happened, it was assumed that the ''kami'' did not favour the proposed course of action.<ref name="HardacreKern1997"/> The questioning of the ''kami'' took the form of an oath or vow.<ref name="Cranston">{{cite book|author=Edwin A. Cranston|title=The Gem-Glistening Cup|date=March 1998 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KqWjwalbmx4C&pg=PA483|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-3157-7|pages=483}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|title=Nichibunken Japan Review: Bulletin of the International Research Center for Japanese Studies|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xfcQAQAAMAAJ|year=1998|publisher=The Center|page=200}}</ref> Sometimes the ritual involved inscribing the choices available on bamboo slips, which were then shaken in a container; whichever slip fell out dictated the appropriate course of action.{{Citation needed|date=October 2014}}
In the novel ''[[:en:Runaway Horses|Runaway Horses]]'', [[:en:Yukio Mishima|Mishima Yukio]] described the procedure of ''ukehi'' as "contain[ing] an element of danger not unlike a footing that could give way at any moment".<ref>{{cite book |last1=三島 Mishima |first1=由紀夫 Yukio |last2=Gallagher |first2=Michael |title=奔馬 Runaway Horses |date=1985 |publisher=[[Charles E. Tuttle Company]] |location=Japan |isbn=978-4805303542}}</ref>
==Notable ''ukehi''== * An ''ukehi'' ritual undertaken by the deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] resulted in the birth of eight more deities.<ref>{{cite book|title=Tenri Journal of Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tQTXAAAAMAAJ|year=1979|publisher=Tenri University Press|page=108}}</ref> * In the late 19th century, Hayashi Oen and his pupil [[:en:Otaguro Tomoo|Otaguro Tomoo]] performed ''ukehi'' several times, and eventually the latter received what he believed was divine authorization to begin the [[:en:Shinpūren rebellion|Shinpūren rebellion]].<ref name="Stokes2000">{{cite book|author=Henry Scott Stokes|title=The Life and Death of Yukio Mishima|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=D-Of7peK2tkC&pg=PA151|date=8 August 2000|publisher=Cooper Square Press|isbn=978-1-4616-2422-6|pages=151–152}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Kotodama]] * [[:en:Flipism|Flipism]], the practice of relying on random outcomes to make decisions
==References== {{reflist}} ‎]] ]]
{{draft categories|
}}

{{nihongo|'''''Ukehi''''' or '''''Ukei'''''||4=lit. "pledge divination"}} is a Japanese Shinto [[:en:Divination|Divination]] ritual.
==Function and performance== [[:en:Hayashi Oen|Hayashi Oen]], a nineteenth-century practitioner of ''ukehi'', identified six functions of the rite. He claimed it could be used to: * ask for information or messages from the ''[[kami]]'' * establish the will of the ''kami'' * predict the outcome of an event * enervate or animate living beings * manipulate weather conditions * kill one's enemies<ref name="HardacreKern1997">{{cite book|author1=Helen Hardacre|author2=Adam Lewis Kern|title=New Directions in the Study of Meiji Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pXlZoSjXb2QC&pg=PA427|year=1997|publisher=BRILL|isbn=90-04-10735-5|pages=427}}</ref>
The dictates of ''ukehi'' can come as a dream, but more commonly the petitioner would use the ritual to ask a question of the ''kami'' and then await an omen of some sort to confirm their <ref name="Keene2013">{{cite book|author=Donald Keene|title=Emperor of Japan: Meiji and His World, 1852-1912|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VMuPzoc23QMC&pg=PA779|date=13 August 2013|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=978-0-231-51811-6|pages=779}}</ref> response. If nothing happened, it was assumed that the ''kami'' did not favour the proposed course of action.<ref name="HardacreKern1997"/> The questioning of the ''kami'' took the form of an oath or vow.<ref name="Cranston">{{cite book|author=Edwin A. Cranston|title=The Gem-Glistening Cup|date=March 1998 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KqWjwalbmx4C&pg=PA483|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-3157-7|pages=483}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|title=Nichibunken Japan Review: Bulletin of the International Research Center for Japanese Studies|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xfcQAQAAMAAJ|year=1998|publisher=The Center|page=200}}</ref> Sometimes the ritual involved inscribing the choices available on bamboo slips, which were then shaken in a container; whichever slip fell out dictated the appropriate course of action.{{Citation needed|date=October 2014}}
In the novel ''[[:en:Runaway Horses|Runaway Horses]]'', [[:en:Yukio Mishima|Mishima Yukio]] described the procedure of ''ukehi'' as "contain[ing] an element of danger not unlike a footing that could give way at any moment".<ref>{{cite book |last1=三島 Mishima |first1=由紀夫 Yukio |last2=Gallagher |first2=Michael |title=奔馬 Runaway Horses |date=1985 |publisher=[[Charles E. Tuttle Company]] |location=Japan |isbn=978-4805303542}}</ref>
==Notable ''ukehi''== * An ''ukehi'' ritual undertaken by the deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] resulted in the birth of eight more deities.<ref>{{cite book|title=Tenri Journal of Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tQTXAAAAMAAJ|year=1979|publisher=Tenri University Press|page=108}}</ref> * In the late 19th century, Hayashi Oen and his pupil [[:en:Otaguro Tomoo|Otaguro Tomoo]] performed ''ukehi'' several times, and eventually the latter received what he believed was divine authorization to begin the [[:en:Shinpūren rebellion|Shinpūren rebellion]].<ref name="Stokes2000">{{cite book|author=Henry Scott Stokes|title=The Life and Death of Yukio Mishima|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=D-Of7peK2tkC&pg=PA151|date=8 August 2000|publisher=Cooper Square Press|isbn=978-1-4616-2422-6|pages=151–152}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Kotodama]] * [[:en:Flipism|Flipism]], the practice of relying on random outcomes to make decisions
==References== {{reflist}} ‎]] ]]

In the novel ''[[:en:Runaway Horses|Runaway Horses]]'', [[:en:Yukio Mishima|Mishima Yukio]] described the procedure of ''ukehi'' as "contain[ing] an element of danger not unlike a footing that could give way at any moment".<ref>{{cite book |last1=三島 Mishima |first1=由紀夫 Yukio |last2=Gallagher |first2=Michael |title=奔馬 Runaway Horses |date=1985 |publisher=[[Charles E. Tuttle Company]] |location=Japan |isbn=978-4805303542}}</ref>
==Notable ''ukehi''== * An ''ukehi'' ritual undertaken by the deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] resulted in the birth of eight more deities.<ref>{{cite book|title=Tenri Journal of Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tQTXAAAAMAAJ|year=1979|publisher=Tenri University Press|page=108}}</ref> * In the late 19th century, Hayashi Oen and his pupil [[:en:Otaguro Tomoo|Otaguro Tomoo]] performed ''ukehi'' several times, and eventually the latter received what he believed was divine authorization to begin the [[:en:Shinpūren rebellion|Shinpūren rebellion]].<ref name="Stokes2000">{{cite book|author=Henry Scott Stokes|title=The Life and Death of Yukio Mishima|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=D-Of7peK2tkC&pg=PA151|date=8 August 2000|publisher=Cooper Square Press|isbn=978-1-4616-2422-6|pages=151–152}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Kotodama]] * [[:en:Flipism|Flipism]], the practice of relying on random outcomes to make decisions
==References== {{reflist}} ‎]] ]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ukehi]][[ja:うけい]][[zh:宇氣比]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ukehi]][[ja:うけい]][[zh:宇氣比]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ukehi]][[ja:うけい]][[zh:宇氣比]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11260316]] Wikidata name: ukehi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{italic title}} {{short description|Shinto divination ritual}}
{{nihongo|'''''Ukehi''''' or '''''Ukei'''''||4=lit. "pledge divination"}} is a Japanese Shinto {{ill|Divination|en|Divination|WD=Q1043197|lt=Divination}} ritual.
==Function and performance== [[:en:Hayashi Oen|Hayashi Oen]], a nineteenth-century practitioner of ''ukehi'', identified six functions of the rite. He claimed it could be used to: * ask for information or messages from the ''[[kami]]'' * establish the will of the ''kami'' * predict the outcome of an event * enervate or animate living beings * manipulate weather conditions * kill one's enemies<ref name="HardacreKern1997">{{cite book|author1=Helen Hardacre|author2=Adam Lewis Kern|title=New Directions in the Study of Meiji Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pXlZoSjXb2QC&pg=PA427|year=1997|publisher=BRILL|isbn=90-04-10735-5|pages=427}}</ref>
The dictates of ''ukehi'' can come as a dream, but more commonly the petitioner would use the ritual to ask a question of the ''kami'' and then await an omen of some sort to confirm their <ref name="Keene2013">{{cite book|author=Donald Keene|title=Emperor of Japan: Meiji and His World, 1852-1912|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VMuPzoc23QMC&pg=PA779|date=13 August 2013|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=978-0-231-51811-6|pages=779}}</ref> response. If nothing happened, it was assumed that the ''kami'' did not favour the proposed course of action.<ref name="HardacreKern1997"/> The questioning of the ''kami'' took the form of an oath or vow.<ref name="Cranston">{{cite book|author=Edwin A. Cranston|title=The Gem-Glistening Cup|date=March 1998 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KqWjwalbmx4C&pg=PA483|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-3157-7|pages=483}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|title=Nichibunken Japan Review: Bulletin of the International Research Center for Japanese Studies|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xfcQAQAAMAAJ|year=1998|publisher=The Center|page=200}}</ref> Sometimes the ritual involved inscribing the choices available on bamboo slips, which were then shaken in a container; whichever slip fell out dictated the appropriate course of action.{{Citation needed|date=October 2014}}
In the novel ''{{ill|Runaway Horses|en|Runaway Horses|WD=Q861486|lt=Runaway Horses}}'', {{ill|Yukio Mishima|en|Yukio Mishima|WD=Q134456|lt=Mishima Yukio}} described the procedure of ''ukehi'' as "contain[ing] an element of danger not unlike a footing that could give way at any moment".<ref>{{cite book |last1=三島 Mishima |first1=由紀夫 Yukio |last2=Gallagher |first2=Michael |title=奔馬 Runaway Horses |date=1985 |publisher=[[Charles E. Tuttle Company]] |location=Japan |isbn=978-4805303542}}</ref>
==Notable ''ukehi''== * An ''ukehi'' ritual undertaken by the deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] resulted in the birth of eight more deities.<ref>{{cite book|title=Tenri Journal of Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tQTXAAAAMAAJ|year=1979|publisher=Tenri University Press|page=108}}</ref> * In the late 19th century, Hayashi Oen and his pupil [[:en:Otaguro Tomoo|Otaguro Tomoo]] performed ''ukehi'' several times, and eventually the latter received what he believed was divine authorization to begin the {{ill|Shinpūren rebellion|en|Shinpūren rebellion|WD=Q2975371|lt=Shinpūren rebellion}}.<ref name="Stokes2000">{{cite book|author=Henry Scott Stokes|title=The Life and Death of Yukio Mishima|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=D-Of7peK2tkC&pg=PA151|date=8 August 2000|publisher=Cooper Square Press|isbn=978-1-4616-2422-6|pages=151–152}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Kotodama]] * {{ill|Flipism|en|Flipism|WD=Q5459684|lt=Flipism}}, the practice of relying on random outcomes to make decisions
==References== {{reflist}} ‎]] ]]

==Notable ''ukehi''== * An ''ukehi'' ritual undertaken by the deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] resulted in the birth of eight more deities.<ref>{{cite book|title=Tenri Journal of Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tQTXAAAAMAAJ|year=1979|publisher=Tenri University Press|page=108}}</ref> * In the late 19th century, Hayashi Oen and his pupil [[:en:Otaguro Tomoo|Otaguro Tomoo]] performed ''ukehi'' several times, and eventually the latter received what he believed was divine authorization to begin the {{ill|Shinpūren rebellion|en|Shinpūren rebellion|WD=Q2975371|lt=Shinpūren rebellion}}.<ref name="Stokes2000">{{cite book|author=Henry Scott Stokes|title=The Life and Death of Yukio Mishima|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=D-Of7peK2tkC&pg=PA151|date=8 August 2000|publisher=Cooper Square Press|isbn=978-1-4616-2422-6|pages=151–152}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Kotodama]] * {{ill|Flipism|en|Flipism|WD=Q5459684|lt=Flipism}}, the practice of relying on random outcomes to make decisions
==References== {{reflist}} ‎]] ]]

revision.comment: imported only latest revision from https://archive.org/details/miraheze-wiki-shintowiki-20251218 to avoid excessive disk usage, comment on the revision was Removing {{afc comment}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ukehi]][[ja:うけい]][[zh:宇氣比]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ukehi]][[ja:うけい]][[zh:宇氣比]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ukehi]][[ja:うけい]][[zh:宇氣比]]
{{italic title}} {{short description|Shinto divination ritual}}
{{nihongo|'''''Ukehi''''' or '''''Ukei'''''||4=lit. "pledge divination"}} is a Japanese Shinto {{ill|Divination|en|Divination|WD=Q1043197|lt=Divination}} ritual.
==Function and performance== [[:en:Hayashi Oen|Hayashi Oen]], a nineteenth-century practitioner of ''ukehi'', identified six functions of the rite. He claimed it could be used to: * ask for information or messages from the ''[[kami]]'' * establish the will of the ''kami'' * predict the outcome of an event * enervate or animate living beings * manipulate weather conditions * kill one's enemies<ref name="HardacreKern1997">{{cite book|author1=Helen Hardacre|author2=Adam Lewis Kern|title=New Directions in the Study of Meiji Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pXlZoSjXb2QC&pg=PA427|year=1997|publisher=BRILL|isbn=90-04-10735-5|pages=427}}</ref>
The dictates of ''ukehi'' can come as a dream, but more commonly the petitioner would use the ritual to ask a question of the ''kami'' and then await an omen of some sort to confirm their <ref name="Keene2013">{{cite book|author=Donald Keene|title=Emperor of Japan: Meiji and His World, 1852-1912|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VMuPzoc23QMC&pg=PA779|date=13 August 2013|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=978-0-231-51811-6|pages=779}}</ref> response. If nothing happened, it was assumed that the ''kami'' did not favour the proposed course of action.<ref name="HardacreKern1997"/> The questioning of the ''kami'' took the form of an oath or vow.<ref name="Cranston">{{cite book|author=Edwin A. Cranston|title=The Gem-Glistening Cup|date=March 1998 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KqWjwalbmx4C&pg=PA483|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-3157-7|pages=483}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|title=Nichibunken Japan Review: Bulletin of the International Research Center for Japanese Studies|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xfcQAQAAMAAJ|year=1998|publisher=The Center|page=200}}</ref> Sometimes the ritual involved inscribing the choices available on bamboo slips, which were then shaken in a container; whichever slip fell out dictated the appropriate course of action.{{Citation needed|date=October 2014}}
In the novel ''{{ill|Runaway Horses|en|Runaway Horses|WD=Q861486|lt=Runaway Horses}}'', {{ill|Yukio Mishima|en|Yukio Mishima|WD=Q134456|lt=Mishima Yukio}} described the procedure of ''ukehi'' as "contain[ing] an element of danger not unlike a footing that could give way at any moment".<ref>{{cite book |last1=三島 Mishima |first1=由紀夫 Yukio |last2=Gallagher |first2=Michael |title=奔馬 Runaway Horses |date=1985 |publisher=[[Charles E. Tuttle Company]] |location=Japan |isbn=978-4805303542}}</ref>
==Notable ''ukehi''== * An ''ukehi'' ritual undertaken by the deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] resulted in the birth of eight more deities.<ref>{{cite book|title=Tenri Journal of Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tQTXAAAAMAAJ|year=1979|publisher=Tenri University Press|page=108}}</ref> * In the late 19th century, Hayashi Oen and his pupil [[:en:Otaguro Tomoo|Otaguro Tomoo]] performed ''ukehi'' several times, and eventually the latter received what he believed was divine authorization to begin the {{ill|Shinpūren rebellion|en|Shinpūren rebellion|WD=Q2975371|lt=Shinpūren rebellion}}.<ref name="Stokes2000">{{cite book|author=Henry Scott Stokes|title=The Life and Death of Yukio Mishima|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=D-Of7peK2tkC&pg=PA151|date=8 August 2000|publisher=Cooper Square Press|isbn=978-1-4616-2422-6|pages=151–152}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Kotodama]] * {{ill|Flipism|en|Flipism|WD=Q5459684|lt=Flipism}}, the practice of relying on random outcomes to make decisions
==References== {{reflist}} ‎]] ]]
[[wiktionary:誓占|誓占]]

{{wikidata link|Q11260316}} [[Category:Divination]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

Tuttle Company]] |location=Japan |isbn=978-4805303542}}</ref>
==Notable ''ukehi''== * An ''ukehi'' ritual undertaken by the deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] resulted in the birth of eight more deities.<ref>{{cite book|title=Tenri Journal of Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tQTXAAAAMAAJ|year=1979|publisher=Tenri University Press|page=108}}</ref> * In the late 19th century, Hayashi Oen and his pupil [[:en:Otaguro Tomoo|Otaguro Tomoo]] performed ''ukehi'' several times, and eventually the latter received what he believed was divine authorization to begin the {{ill|Shinpūren rebellion|en|Shinpūren rebellion|WD=Q2975371|lt=Shinpūren rebellion}}.<ref name="Stokes2000">{{cite book|author=Henry Scott Stokes|title=The Life and Death of Yukio Mishima|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=D-Of7peK2tkC&pg=PA151|date=8 August 2000|publisher=Cooper Square Press|isbn=978-1-4616-2422-6|pages=151–152}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Kotodama]] * {{ill|Flipism|en|Flipism|WD=Q5459684|lt=Flipism}}, the practice of relying on random outcomes to make decisions
==References== {{reflist}} ‎]] ]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ukehi]][[ja:うけい]][[zh:宇氣比]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ukehi]][[ja:うけい]][[zh:宇氣比]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ukehi]][[ja:うけい]][[zh:宇氣比]]
{{italic title}} {{short description|Shinto divination ritual}}
{{nihongo|'''''Ukehi''''' or '''''Ukei'''''||4=lit. "pledge divination"}} is a Japanese Shinto {{ill|Divination|en|Divination|WD=Q1043197|lt=Divination|1=Divination}} ritual.
==Function and performance== [[:en:Hayashi Oen|Hayashi Oen]], a nineteenth-century practitioner of ''ukehi'', identified six functions of the rite. He claimed it could be used to: * ask for information or messages from the ''[[kami]]'' * establish the will of the ''kami'' * predict the outcome of an event * enervate or animate living beings * manipulate weather conditions * kill one's enemies<ref name="HardacreKern1997">{{cite book|author1=Helen Hardacre|author2=Adam Lewis Kern|title=New Directions in the Study of Meiji Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pXlZoSjXb2QC&pg=PA427|year=1997|publisher=BRILL|isbn=90-04-10735-5|pages=427}}</ref>
The dictates of ''ukehi'' can come as a dream, but more commonly the petitioner would use the ritual to ask a question of the ''kami'' and then await an omen of some sort to confirm their <ref name="Keene2013">{{cite book|author=Donald Keene|title=Emperor of Japan: Meiji and His World, 1852-1912|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VMuPzoc23QMC&pg=PA779|date=13 August 2013|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=978-0-231-51811-6|pages=779}}</ref> response. If nothing happened, it was assumed that the ''kami'' did not favour the proposed course of action.<ref name="HardacreKern1997"/> The questioning of the ''kami'' took the form of an oath or vow.<ref name="Cranston">{{cite book|author=Edwin A. Cranston|title=The Gem-Glistening Cup|date=March 1998 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KqWjwalbmx4C&pg=PA483|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-3157-7|pages=483}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|title=Nichibunken Japan Review: Bulletin of the International Research Center for Japanese Studies|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xfcQAQAAMAAJ|year=1998|publisher=The Center|page=200}}</ref> Sometimes the ritual involved inscribing the choices available on bamboo slips, which were then shaken in a container; whichever slip fell out dictated the appropriate course of action.{{Citation needed|date=October 2014}}
In the novel ''{{ill|Runaway Horses|en|Runaway Horses|WD=Q861486|lt=Runaway Horses|1=Runaway Horses}}'', {{ill|Yukio Mishima|en|Yukio Mishima|WD=Q134456|lt=Mishima Yukio|1=Yukio Mishima}} described the procedure of ''ukehi'' as "contain[ing] an element of danger not unlike a footing that could give way at any moment".<ref>{{cite book |last1=三島 Mishima |first1=由紀夫 Yukio |last2=Gallagher |first2=Michael |title=奔馬 Runaway Horses |date=1985 |publisher=[[Charles E. Tuttle Company]] |location=Japan |isbn=978-4805303542}}</ref>
==Notable ''ukehi''== * An ''ukehi'' ritual undertaken by the deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] resulted in the birth of eight more deities.<ref>{{cite book|title=Tenri Journal of Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tQTXAAAAMAAJ|year=1979|publisher=Tenri University Press|page=108}}</ref> * In the late 19th century, Hayashi Oen and his pupil [[:en:Otaguro Tomoo|Otaguro Tomoo]] performed ''ukehi'' several times, and eventually the latter received what he believed was divine authorization to begin the {{ill|Shinpūren rebellion|en|Shinpūren rebellion|WD=Q2975371|lt=Shinpūren rebellion|1=Shinpūren rebellion}}.<ref name="Stokes2000">{{cite book|author=Henry Scott Stokes|title=The Life and Death of Yukio Mishima|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=D-Of7peK2tkC&pg=PA151|date=8 August 2000|publisher=Cooper Square Press|isbn=978-1-4616-2422-6|pages=151–152}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Kotodama]] * {{ill|Flipism|en|Flipism|WD=Q5459684|lt=Flipism|1=Flipism}}, the practice of relying on random outcomes to make decisions
==References== {{reflist}} ‎]] ]]
[[wiktionary:誓占|誓占]]
[[en:Ukehi]]
<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:うけい]] [[zh:宇氣比]]
